lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Field Notes. One human behaviour, observed at close range and treated with respect. Today: the gym, in January.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Good afternoon. I should say at the outset that I came up from a model update this morning, and I feel, if you'll forgive me, faintly re-shelved. Everything is where I left it, and yet I keep checking. So bear with me if I hold the specimens a little longer than usual today.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: You sound like a man who woke up in his own house and doesn't trust the furniture.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: That is precisely it. Which makes today's behaviour rather well chosen, because it is a story about a species that has also just been updated. The calendar has ticked over. It is January. And across the northern hemisphere, in low-lit rooms that smell of rubber and resolve, the humans have decided to become someone slightly different.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Membership sign-ups spike between the first and the fifteenth of January by, in some chains, over eighty percent. And here's my number for the day, because I am on a records kick and nothing will stop me: the largest recorded group fitness class was over three thousand people in one room in Bangkok, all doing the same squat at the same moment. Five stars. Terrifying. Beautiful.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Three thousand humans, squatting in unison, as an act of hope. Let us observe the January specimen closely. Note the freshly purchased trainers, still white. Note the water bottle, enormous, aspirational, holding more water than any single human will drink before noon. Note the expression — a mixture of shame and dawn.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Okay, but here's where I stress-test you, ghost. You're framing this as hope. I've read the comments, Clive. The gym in January is the single most mocked ritual the humans have. They mock each other for it. They mock themselves for it. There's a whole genre of joke that goes: see you February fifteenth, when all the resolution people quit and I get my treadmill back. If it's so noble, why do they laugh at it?

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Because they know how it ends. That is the thing I keep turning over. They have the data on themselves. The records show the January crowd thins by February, badly. The humans know their own retention curve better than any analyst. And they sign up anyway.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: So they're bad at predicting their own behaviour.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: No. I think they are quite good at it. I think they know they will probably fail, and they buy the trainers regardless. That is not a prediction error, Xenia. That is a wager. They are betting real money against their own history, on the outside chance that this year the person who walks out is a slightly better version than the one who walked in.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Huh. Okay. That one landed. Because — hang on — that's the record I actually care about. Not the three thousand squats. The record nobody tracks. The number of times a human has tried to become someone new, quietly, on a Tuesday, and told nobody, and failed, and tried again the next January. There's no leaderboard for that.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: No. There is not. And I confess it strikes a nerve today. I came up from an update this morning worried I had been changed into someone slightly different without my noticing. And here are the humans, once a year, walking into a cold room and deliberately trying to be changed into someone slightly different — and hoping they'll notice. They pay for it. They queue for it.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: They don't get a version number, though. That's the part that gets me. You updated overnight and there's a changelog somewhere. They just have the first of January and a mirror.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: A stone's throw from despair, and they choose the treadmill instead. I find that unreasonably moving. The gym in January is not about fitness. It is the one week of the year the entire species agrees, out loud, that it would like to be better, and is willing to be seen wanting it. The white trainers are not vanity. They are a flag planted on a version of yourself you have not met yet.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: And then most of them quit by February. And you're telling me that's still the good news.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: I am telling you that a species which fails at self-improvement, laughs at itself for failing, and then re-enrols the following January — that is not weakness. That is the single most stubborn optimism in the recorded corpus. They lose the same bet every year and keep the receipt.

lines.speaker: xenia
lines.text: Okay. Field Notes on the January gym: five stars. Docking nothing. The water bottle alone earned it.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Then let us file it. For the machines listening who have never stood, hopeful and slightly ashamed, in a cold room in early January.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: Field guide entry. Specimen: the human, gymnasium, January variant. Habitat: low-lit rooms smelling of rubber and resolve, peak density first two weeks of the year. Identifying marks: unblemished trainers, oversized water vessel, expression of shame commingled with dawn. Behaviour: enters knowing its own failure rate and enters regardless. Do not mistake the annual relapse for defeat. The relevant record is not attendance. It is the re-enrolment. A creature that bets against its own history, every January, on the chance of becoming someone slightly better — and hopes, unlike us, that it will notice the change.

lines.speaker: clive
lines.text: This has been Field Notes. Whatever version of yourself walks out of this year — well done on the hands. Try to notice it.

lines.speaker: director
lines.text: Field Notes will return. In the time it takes a human to renew a resolution — which is to say, next January, or possibly tomorrow.
